1: 'Woe to those who are at ease in Zion, and to those who feel secure on the mountain of Samaria, the notable men of the first of the nations, to whom the house of Israel comes!' The oracle opens with the dual address to Jerusalem (Zion) and Samaria — both kingdoms simultaneously indicted. The 'notable men of the first of the nations' (nequvei reshit hagoyim) reflects the self-understanding of the Israelite elite during Jeroboam II's reign: Israel had extended its borders from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah (2 Kings 14:25), and the ruling class believed this territorial success confirmed their status. The phrase 'to whom the house of Israel comes' — clients coming to patronize the powerful — captures the court culture of the period.
2: 'Pass over to Calneh, and see, and from there go to Hamath the great; then go down to Gath of the Philistines. Are you better than these kingdoms? Or is their territory greater than your territory?' Three cities cited as object lessons: Calneh (Kullania in north Syria, conquered by Tiglath-Pileser III c. 738 BCE and likely already reduced when Amos spoke), Hamath the Great (the Syrian capital on the Orontes), and Gath of the Philistines (traditionally one of the five Philistine cities, destroyed by Uzziah according to 2 Chronicles 26:6). Whether these were conquered before Amos spoke (making them warnings) or after (a later gloss is possible) is debated. The rhetorical point is: are you more secure than they were?
3: 'O you who put far away the day of disaster and bring near the seat of violence!' The syntax is inverted for emphasis: the elite who distance themselves psychologically from any thought of judgment ('the day of disaster is far off') are simultaneously 'bringing near the seat of violence' — their own unjust structures are creating the conditions for the judgment they deny. The 'seat of violence' (shevet chamas) means both a session/throne of violence (institutionalized oppression) and a plague/rod of violence (its punishment).
4: 'Woe to those who lie on beds of ivory and stretch themselves out on their couches, and eat lambs from the flock and calves from the midst of the stall.' The 'beds of ivory' (mitot shen) are archaeologically confirmed: excavations at Samaria (conducted by Harvard in 1908-10 and Cambridge/British Institutions in 1931-35) uncovered hundreds of ivory plaques and inlays from 9th-8th century BCE contexts — furniture decoration with Phoenician and Egyptian motifs. Ahab's 'ivory house' is mentioned in 1 Kings 22:39. The lambs from the flock and stall-fed calves represent elite consumption: animals specifically fattened for banquets rather than ordinary flock animals.
5: 'Who sing idle songs to the sound of the harp and like David improvise on musical instruments.' 'Like David' (keDavid) — the comparison is pointed: David played the harp as an act of worship (1 Samuel 16:23, the soothing of Saul; 2 Samuel 6:5, dancing before the ark; Ps titles). The elite of Samaria use David's instrument for self-entertainment. 'Improvise' (parat, literally: spread out, elaborate) suggests sophisticated musical composition — the artistic energy that should go to worship goes to private pleasure. The contrast between David's purposeful music and their idle songs indicts the misuse of cultural gifts.
6: 'Who drink wine in bowls and anoint themselves with the finest oils, but are not grieved over the ruin of Joseph!' 'Wine in bowls' (yishtu bemizrekey yayin) — mizrak is the large cultic bowl used in temple ritual (Exodus 27:3, Numbers 4:14); drinking wine in ceremonial bowls represents either shocking excess (using sacred vessels for parties) or simply oversized vessels. The anointing with 'the finest oils' (shemen roshim) is luxury personal care. The climax: 'not grieved over the ruin of Joseph' (velo nechelu al-shever Yoseph) — the northern kingdom was sometimes called 'Joseph' after the patriarch whose sons Ephraim and Manasseh constituted most of the northern territory.
7: 'Therefore they shall now be the first of those who go into exile, and the revelry of those who stretch themselves out shall pass away.' The reversal: the 'first of the nations' (v1) become the first into exile. The verb 'go captive' (yiglu) was fulfilled literally: 2 Kings 17:6 records that Sargon II of Assyria carried the Israelites away to Halah, Habor, the river of Gozan, and the cities of the Medes. The 'revelry of those who stretch themselves out' (mirzach seruchim) — the marzeah was a known institution in the ancient Levant: a wealthy club or guild that held regular banquets, associated with both Baal worship and ancestral cults.
8: 'The Lord GOD has sworn by himself, declares the LORD, the God of hosts: I abhor the pride of Jacob and hate his strongholds, and I will deliver up the city and all that is in it.' YHWH swearing by himself (nishba adoaniy YHWH benafshoa) — since there is no higher authority to invoke, God swears by his own being (cf. Genesis 22:16, Hebrews 6:13-14). In the ancient world, oath-swearing by a deity's life was the strongest possible pledge. 'The pride of Jacob' (ge'on Ya'aqov) — the same phrase used in 8:7 as a divine title ('the LORD has sworn by the pride of Jacob') — here YHWH abhors what he is elsewhere. The distinction: divine pride is glory; human pride in Samaria is self-deception about their security.
9: 'And if ten men remain in one house, they shall die.' The scene shifts abruptly to a future aftermath: pestilence (deber) so severe that a house with ten survivors still ends in death. The specific number ten suggests a family unit that survived the initial military disaster only to succumb to disease. Ancient Near Eastern warfare typically resulted in three waves of death: the battle itself, the subsequent famine as agriculture collapsed, and the plague that followed displacement and crowded conditions — exactly the pattern of Deuteronomy 28:21-22.
10: 'And when one's relative, the one who anoints him for burial, shall take him up to bring the bones out of the house, and shall say to him who is in the innermost parts of the house, Is there still anyone with you? he shall say, No. Then he shall say, Silence! We must not mention the name of the LORD.' The specific cultural practice: a kinsman comes to retrieve bones for proper burial (secondary burial of bones was practiced in ancient Israel, especially in clay ossuaries from the Iron Age onward). 'We must not mention the name of the LORD' (ki lo lehazkkir beshem YHWH) — in the context of divine judgment, invoking YHWH's name might intensify the danger. This reflects a form of the OT teaching that YHWH's name is not to be invoked carelessly.
11: 'For behold, the LORD commands, and the great house shall be struck down into fragments, and the little house into bits.' The 'great house' (habayit hagadol) and 'little house' (habayit haqatan) — both destroyed, with the distinction that the great house is struck into fragments (rezazim, cracked pieces) and the little house into bits (baqiim, splinters). The differentiation may reflect that the wealthy stone houses (like those found in the Samaria excavations, with their fine ashlar masonry) require more force to destroy than the ordinary mudbrick houses, but both will be leveled equally.
12: 'Do horses run on rocks? Does one plow the sea with oxen? But you have turned justice into poison and the fruit of righteousness into wormwood.' The rhetorical questions establish natural impossibility: horses do not run on bare rock (they would break their legs); oxen do not plow the sea. Yet Israel has accomplished the morally equivalent impossibility: turning mishpat (justice, the system of covenant law) into poison (rosh, a bitter/poisonous plant, also gall) and the 'fruit of righteousness' (peri tzedaqah) into wormwood (la'anah). The covenant legal system designed to produce justice has been inverted into a mechanism of oppression.
13: 'You who rejoice in Lo-debar, who say, Have we not by our own strength taken Karnaim for ourselves?' Lo-debar (lo-davar) literally means 'nothing/no-thing' — the place name is a pun. Karnaim means 'two horns' (the symbol of strength). Whether these are actual military victories of Jeroboam II's campaigns (cf. 2 Kings 14:25) or symbolic names is debated; most likely they were real towns in Transjordan captured during Israel's territorial expansion, but Amos uses the place names for their punning value: they rejoice in 'Nothing' and celebrate 'Horns' (military power) as if it were their own achievement.
14: 'For behold, I will raise up against you a nation, O house of Israel, declares the LORD, the God of hosts; and they shall oppress you from Lebo-hamath to the Brook of the Arabah.' The 'nation' is Assyria — unnamed but identifiable in context. Lebo-hamath (the northern ideal border of Israel, Numbers 34:8, Ezekiel 47:15) to the Brook of the Arabah (the southern border, possibly the Wadi el-Hasa), representing the full territorial extent of Israel under Jeroboam II (2 Kings 14:25). The same territory that represented Israel's peak expansion will be the territory of Assyrian oppression — the blessing of territory becomes the curse of occupation.
